articles.title: You Can Get Better from Prediabetes Without Losing Weight
articles.summary: A new study shows that people with prediabetes can get better without losing weight. Prediabetes means blood sugar is high but not yet diabetes. For a long time, doctors told people to lose weight to prevent diabetes. But many find it hard to lose weight and feel sad. The study found that about one in four people in lifestyle programs got normal blood sugar without losing weight. This happens because where fat is in the body matters more than weight. Bad fat around organs can make blood sugar high, while fat under the skin can help. Also, hormones in the body can help control blood sugar. This news could change how doctors help people at risk of diabetes.

articles.title: Wildfires in Tropical Peatlands Reach Highest Level in 2000 Years
articles.summary: A new study shows that wildfires in tropical peatlands have increased a lot in the 20th century, reaching the highest level in 2000 years. Peatlands are special wetlands that store a huge amount of carbon underground, more than all the forests in the world. When they burn, they release this carbon into the air, which can make global warming worse. Scientists looked at old charcoal in peat from places like Southeast Asia and Africa to see how fires happened long ago. They found that fires used to be linked to droughts and climate, but they decreased for over 1000 years. Then, in the 1900s, fires started rising sharply because of human activities like draining peatlands for farming and clearing forests. This is especially bad in Southeast Asia. The study says we need to protect these peatlands to stop more carbon from being released and help fight climate change.

articles.title: Scientists Solve Dinosaur Egg Mystery with a Model Nest
articles.summary: Scientists in Taiwan built a life-size model of an oviraptor dinosaur and its nest to learn how these creatures hatched their eggs 70 million years ago. Oviraptors were bird-like but flightless dinosaurs that lived in China. They laid eggs in rings in open nests. The researchers used materials like foam and resin to make the model and tested how heat moved through the eggs. They found that when an adult dinosaur was present in cold weather, eggs in the outer ring got much cooler, which could cause them to hatch at different times. In warm weather, sunlight helped keep temperatures more even. This study shows that oviraptors probably used sunlight to warm their eggs, unlike modern birds that sit on them. It helps solve a long mystery about dinosaur parenting.

articles.title: Cats and People: A Surprising Cancer Connection
articles.summary: Scientists studied cancer in cats and found it can help people too. They looked at tumors from nearly 500 pet cats in five countries. Cats live with us, so they face similar risks like bad things in the environment. The study showed that cat cancers have genetic changes that are very much like those in human cancers. For example, cat breast cancer is similar to human breast cancer. This means doctors can learn from cats to make better treatments for both. The research created a big map of cat cancer genes, which is a first. They found key genes like FBXW7 and PIK3CA that are important in cat and human cancers. This helps scientists understand cancer better and could lead to new medicines.

articles.title: A Coal Plant That's Not Working but Must Stay Open
articles.summary: In the United States, a coal power plant in Washington was supposed to close and change to natural gas, but the government ordered it to stay open to help keep electricity reliable. However, the plant is barely producing any power, making only a tiny amount of electricity in two months, which is like what solar panels on a house can make in eight months. This means the plant is mostly just sitting there, ready to turn on if needed, but not really helping right now. People who pay for electricity are still charged for this plant, even though it doesn't seem necessary. Some lawmakers are trying to make a new law to stop such orders in the future, but it might not pass because of political disagreements. The article shows how decisions about energy can be confusing and costly.

articles.title: Neanderthals Used Birch Tar as Medicine
articles.summary: Neanderthals were ancient people who lived long ago. They used birch tar, a sticky substance from birch trees, not just as glue for tools but also as medicine. Birch tar helped them fight infections from cuts and wounds. Modern Indigenous groups like the Mi'kmaq in Canada still use birch tar for healing. Scientists tested birch tar against bacteria and found it can stop some germs, like Staphylococcus aureus, which causes skin infections. This shows Neanderthals were smart and knew how to use natural things to stay healthy. They made birch tar by heating birch bark, and it worked like an antiseptic to clean wounds.
